title: PDF Page 1
content:
Workbook Tab 1 Instructor’s Case - Instructions 
CHANGED PRODUCT RULE 
INSTRUCTOR’S CASE  
 
Scenario:  
 
The Type Certificate holder of a twin engine, Transport Category aircraft submits an 
application for 4 concurrent changes.  
 
1. Fuselage Plug (adding 2 rows of seats) 
2. Thrust Bump (3%)  
3. Landing Gear Upgrade 
4. Avionics Upgrade 
 
You are to establish the basis of certification for the changed product by applying the 
Changed Product Rule (CPR). 
 
Product being changed:
 Twin jet engine, Transport Category aircraft  
Existing certification basis  CFR Part 25, Amendment 25-41. 
Standards on date of application: CFR Part 25, Amendment 25-108.  
 
Concepts that will be demonstrated: 
 
Step 1 
♦ Defining the change 
 
♦ Previous relevant changes 
 
Step 2 
♦ Substantial or not 
 
Step 3 
♦ Existing, earlier and latest requirements 
♦ Special conditions 
 
Step 4 
♦ Significant or not 
 
1

title: PDF Page 3
content:
Workbook Tab 1 – Case – Change 1 – Fuselage Length Increase 
1 
WORKSHEET – STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
 
CHANGE 1 – FUSELAGE LENGTH INCREASE 
 
Description of the change: 
 
Lengthen fuselage by 10 feet from 70 to 80 feet, adding 2 rows of 
seats (i.e. 10 additional pax, going from 90-100 pax), no change in 
gross weight. 
Reason for change:  
 
Increase in passenger carrying capacity of aircraft in order to 
increase revenue on short sectors (trading range for payload.) 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
♦ Fuselage plug of 10 ft.; total fuselage 
(12.5%) length is now 80 ft.   
♦ Extend floor 
♦ Add two rows of seats 
♦ 30% increase size of cargo compartment 
 
♦ Lengthen control cable runs  
♦ Add overhead bins  
♦ Extended electrical and mechanical           
circuits (wire, plumbing, ducts etc.) 
 
 
 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply)
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
 
 
 
List any Concurrent related
 changes.  
 
List any Previous relevant changes. Nil

source_url: C:\Users\spars\OneDrive\Desktop\FAA\OneDrive_2026-03-20\TC Aircraft Certification Speciality Course 2011\MAY 2011 Binder 3- Changed Product Rules\Workshop\WS-CPR-TAB_1_INSTRUCTOR_CASE_NOV08.pdf
page_url: C:\Users\spars\OneDrive\Desktop\FAA\OneDrive_2026-03-20\TC Aircraft Certification Speciality Course 2011\MAY 2011 Binder 3- Changed Product Rules\Workshop\WS-CPR-TAB_1_INSTRUCTOR_CASE_NOV08.pdf#page=5
title: PDF Page 5
content:
Workbook Tab 1 – Case – Change 1 – Fuselage Length Increase 
 
WORKSHEET – STEP 4 
SIGNIFICANT or NOT? 
 
 The design change is an example on the AC table? 
 The design change is close to an example on the AC table?  
 The design change is NOT on the AC table, review against the following criteria. 
 
GENERAL CONFIGURATION - Is there a change to the general configuration? 
 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change to the general configuration at the product level that is likely to require a new 
model designation because of the need to distinguish the different product with other 
product models (e.g. performance, interchangeability of major components). 
 
PRINCIPLES OF CONSTRUCTION - Is there a change to the principles of construction? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change at the product level to the materials and/or construction methods that affects 
the overall product’s operating characteristics or inherent strength and would require 
extensive re-investigation to show compliance. Examples of this are a metal to 
composite structure, a piston-propeller to turbine-propeller, or single axle main gear to 
double axle main gear. 
 
ASSUMPTIONS - Have the assumptions used for certification been invalidated? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
Changes to product level assumptions, either design or engineering, associated with 
product development, compliance demonstration, performance or operating envelope 
that by themselves are so different, that the original assumptions are invalidated and 
the existing substantiation cannot be extrapolated to cover the changed product.
 
(1) Design assumptions are made during the development of the type certified 
product and include such criteria as passenger capacity, maximum operating 
altitude, aircraft configuration, flight loads, expected life limits and environmental 
operating range, such as flight into know icing, HIRF, etc. 
(2) Engineering assumptions  include such things as wing loading, structural load 
paths, structural strength and stiffness, basic type of propulsion method, and the 
principles or philosophy of the operat ion of systems. Changes in these 
assumptions require an examination of whether the extent of the change amounts 
to an invalidation of the assumptions includ ed for certification. Where the physics, 
kinematics, or other scientific principles involved in the change are different from 
those applicable to the original product, the assumptions may not remain valid if 
the change cannot be validly analyzed by  extrapolating the analysis from the 
original product. 
Examples may include: 
(a)  Change of an aircraft from an unpr essurized to pressurized fuselage; 
(b)  Change of operation of a fixed wing aircraft from land based to water based; and, 
(c)  Operation envelope expansions that ar e outside the existing design parameters 
and capabilities. 
 
Note:  Merely operating a product to an expanded envelope for which it was originally 
designed is generally not a significant change.  In this case, the assumptions used for 
certification of the basic product remain valid and the results can be applied to cover 
the changed product with predictable effects or can be demonstrated without significant 
physical changes to the product.   
 
 
Calibrate your preliminary decision re Significant or Not Significant using similar examples in the AC tables. 
Conclusion: 
3

content:
Workbook Tab 1 – Case – Change 2 Increased Thrust 
1 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
CHANGE 2 – INCREASED THRUST 
Description of the change: 
 
Increase in engine thrust. 
 
Reason for change:  
 
The change is required in order to take off on shorter runways. 
 
Primary Change (s) Secondary change (s) 
 
♦ 3% thrust bump 
♦  Add additional fire protection 
 
 
 
♦ Nil 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Criteria for Secondary change (all elements must apply)
 
 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
 
 
List any Concurrent related changes.  
 
List any Previous relevant changes. 22% previous increase without update of the 
certification basis.

 The design change is an example on the AC table? 
 The design change is close to an example on the AC table?  
 The design change is NOT on the AC table, review against the following criteria. 
 
GENERAL CONFIGURATION - Is there a change to the general configuration? 
 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change to the general configuration at the product level that is likely to require a new 
model designation because of the need to distinguish the different product with other 
product models (e.g. performance, interchangeability of major components).

Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change at the product level to the materials and/or construction methods that affects

Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
Changes to product level assumptions, either design or engineering, associated with

envelope that by themselves are so different, that the original assumptions are 
invalidated and the existing substantiation cannot be extrapolated to cover the 
changed product. 
 
(1) Design assumptions are made during the development of the type certified 
product and include such criteria as passenger capacity, maximum operating 
altitude, aircraft configuration, flight loads, expected life limits and 
environmental operating range, such as flight into know icing, HIRF, etc. 
(2) Engineering assumptions
 include such things as wing loading, structural load 
paths, structural strength an d stiffness, basic type of propulsion method, and 
the principles or philosophy of the op eration of systems. Changes in these 
assumptions require an examination of  whether the extent of the change 
amounts to an invalidation of the assump tions included for certification. Where 
the physics, kinematics, or other scientific principles involved in the change are 
different from those applicable to the original product, the assumptions may not 
remain valid if the change cannot be validly analyzed by extrapolating the 
analysis from the original product. 
Examples may include: 
(a)  Change of an aircraft from an unpr essurized to pressurized fuselage, 
(b)  Change of operation of a fixed wing aircraft from land based to water based, 
and 
(c)  Operation envelope expansions that ar e outside the existing design parameters 
and capabilities. 
 
Note:  Merely operating a product to an expanded envelope for which it was originally 
designed is generally not a significant change.  In this case, the assumptions used 
for certification of the basic product remain valid and the results can be applied to 
cover the changed product with predictable effects or can be demonstrated without

content:
Workbook Tab 1 – Case – Change 3 Landing Gear Upgrade 
1 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE 
 
CHANGE 3 – LANDING GEAR UPGRADE 
Description of the change: 
 
Landing gear product improvement by upgrading brakes and 
improving maintainability. Number of wheels and axles is 
unchanged. 
Reason for change:  
 
To improve overall function, reliability and maintainability of landing 
gear and reduce costs. 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
♦ Brake model revision to prolong life 
♦ Increase tire plies 16 to 18 
♦ Change oleo strut orifice 
♦  Change in uplock sensors (PSEU) 
♦  Change from cross-ply to radial ply tires 
♦  Add gravel guard option in nose gear 
 
♦ Re-routing of electrical and instrumentation
circuits 
 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply) 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 
 
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product 
 
 
 
List any Concurrent related
 changes.  
 
List any Previous relevant changes. Nil

content:
Workbook Tab 1 – Case – Change 4 Avionics Upgrade 
1 
WORKSHEET- STEP 1 
DEFINING THE CHANGE  
 
 
CHANGE 4 – AVIONICS UPGRADE 
 
Description of the change: 
 
Change 4 - Flight deck avionics update. No reduction in minimum 
flight crew. 
Reason for change:  
 
To take advantage of latest technologies and increase reliability. 
 
Primary change (s) Secondary change (s) 
♦ New Electronic Flight Instrument System 
(EFIS) to replace corresponding mechanical 
instruments  
♦  Minimal changes to the overall configuration 
of the current system – only those changes 
necessary to accommodate new EFIS.  
♦   Flight Management System (FMS) 
installation 
 
 
♦ Wire rerouting 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Criteria for secondary change (all elements must apply) 
Secondary changes are part of the affected area (on the compliance plan) and do require re-
evaluation against the existing standards because they:  
 
 Obviously do not contribute materially to safety by going to the latest standards 
 Are in the realm of “minor” in their own right 
 Are not part of the original intent of the design change (but required by the change) 
 Do not add any new capabilities or capacity to the product

 changes.

page_url: C:\Users\spars\OneDrive\Desktop\FAA\OneDrive_2026-03-20\TC Aircraft Certification Speciality Course 2011\MAY 2011 Binder 3- Changed Product Rules\Workshop\WS-CPR-TAB_1_INSTRUCTOR_CASE_NOV08.pdf#page=17
title: PDF Page 17
content:
Workbook Tab 1 – Case – Change 4 Avionics Upgrade 
 
WORKSHEET 
STEP 4 – SIGNIFICANT or NOT? 
 
 The design change is an example on the AC table? 
 The design change is close to an example on the AC table?  
 The design change is NOT on the AC table, review against the following criteria. 
 
GENERAL CONFIGURATION - Is there a change to the general configuration? 
 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change to the general configuration at the product level that is likely to require a new 
model designation because of the need to distinguish the different product with other 
product models (e.g. performance, interchangeability of major components). 
 
PRINCIPLES OF CONSTRUCTION - Is there a change to the principles of construction? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
A change at the product level to the materials and/or construction methods that affects 
the overall product’s operating characteristics or inherent strength and would require 
extensive re-investigation to show compliance. Examples of this are a metal to 
composite structure, a piston-propeller to turbine-propeller, or single axle main gear to 
double axle main gear. 
 
ASSUMPTIONS - Have the assumptions used for certification been invalidated? 
Definition/criteria Evaluation notes 
Changes to product level assumptions, either design or engineering, associated with 
product development, compliance demonstration, performance or operating envelope 
that by themselves are so different, that the original assumptions are invalidated and 
the existing substantiation cannot be extrapolated to cover the changed product. 
 
(1) Design assumptions are made during the development of the type certified 
product and include such criteria as passenger capacity, maximum operating 
altitude, aircraft configuration, flight loads, expected life limits and environmental 
operating range, such as flight into know icing, HIRF, etc. 
(2) Engineering assumptions  include such things as wing loading, structural load 
paths, structural strength and stiffness, basic type of propulsion method, and the 
principles or philosophy of the operat ion of systems. Changes in these 
assumptions require an examination of whether the extent of the change amounts 
to an invalidation of the assumptions includ ed for certification. Where the physics, 
kinematics, or other scientific principles involved in the change are different from 
those applicable to the original product, the assumptions may not remain valid if 
the change cannot be validly analyzed by  extrapolating the analysis from the 
original product. 
Examples may include: 
(a)  Change of an aircraft from an unpr essurized to pressurized fuselage; 
(b)  Change of operation of a fixed wing aircraft from land based to water based; and, 
(c)  Operation envelope expansions that ar e outside the existing design parameters 
and capabilities. 
 
Note:  Merely operating a product to an expanded envelope for which it was originally 
designed is generally not a significant change.  In this case, the assumptions used for 
certification of the basic product remain valid and the results can be applied to cover 
the changed product with predictable effects or can be demonstrated without significant 
physical changes to the product.   
 
 
Calibrate your preliminary decision of Significant or Not Significant using similar examples in the AC tables. 
Conclusion:
3
